text: Molecular spintronics aims to utilize molecular species as information storage media, which is generally considered to be in the remote future because the current spin qubits are not compatible with the requirements of quantum information science. Alternative spin qubits are therefore needed urgently. Photoexcited chromophore–radical hybrid systems may open a new avenue in this context. Here, we have used transient EPR to investigate three novel molecular hybrids in which a perylene diimide (PDI) chromophore is attached to a stable 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) radical. The influences of different labeling of the TEMPO moiety were systematically investigated, allowing the EPR signals of photoexcited molecular quartet states to be identified. It was possible to show that the spin states can be controlled at room temperature with a spin coherence time (T 2) of 0.7 μs, which is a major milestone on the way to the implementation of molecular qubits in actual quantum devices.
